stories.headline: Artemis II Lifts Off: Humans Are Heading to the Moon for the First Time Since 1972
stories.hook: Four astronauts are currently on their way to the Moon — and for most people alive today, it's the first time that's ever been true.
stories.body:
NASA's Artemis II mission launched successfully this evening from Kennedy Space Center's Pad 39B, sending commander Reid Wiseman, pilot Victor Glover, mission specialist Christina Koch, and Canadian astronaut Jeremy Hansen on a ten-day free-return trajectory around the Moon. It's the first crewed mission beyond low Earth orbit in 54 years, since Apollo 17 departed in December 1972.

The crew is packed with historic firsts: Glover becomes the first person of color to travel to deep space, Koch the first woman, and Hansen the first non-American to venture beyond Earth orbit. On the far side of their loop, they'll directly observe roughly 21% of the lunar surface that no human being has ever seen with their own eyes from space — the far side, forever facing away from Earth.

Artemis II is a critical dress rehearsal for Artemis III, which plans to land astronauts at the Moon's south pole. The mission will stress-test the Orion capsule's life support, communications, and propulsion over the long voyage. For the first time in living memory, human beings are somewhere other than Earth.

image.alt: A view of the cratered surface of the Moon
image.credit: Photo by NASA Hubble Space Telescope on Unsplash

links.title: NASA Artemis II mission overview
Live launch updates — Space.com
CBS News: The astronauts and their mission

stories.headline: A Galaxy 10 Billion Light-Years Away Lost 95% of Its Brightness in Just 20 Years
stories.hook: Something that should take tens of thousands of years happened in the time it takes a child to grow up.
stories.body:
Galaxy J0218-0036 was once blazing with light powered by a supermassive black hole at its center. Then, over roughly two decades, it dimmed to less than one-twentieth of its former brightness — a 95% drop in luminosity that fundamentally defies existing models of how black holes behave. Typical active galactic nuclei flicker by around 30%. This one changed by a factor of 20.

Researchers caught the transformation by comparing archival images from Japan's Subaru Telescope. Their best explanation: the black hole's supply of infalling gas — the fuel that makes a galactic center blaze — appears to have suddenly cut off. But how that happens on a human timescale, rather than the millions of years the physics suggests it should take, remains an open question.

The find is part of a growing class of events called 'changing-look AGN,' where active galaxy cores dramatically brighten or dim faster than theory comfortably allows. Each new case chips away at our confidence that we understand the most energetic objects in the universe. Watching a galaxy effectively switch off, from start to finish, in a single human lifetime is something astronomers have never seen before.

image.alt: A swirling galaxy and bright core against the blackness of space

links.title: Subaru Telescope: Galaxy dramatically dims
Starlust: Supermassive black hole dimmed its galaxy 20-fold

stories.headline: A Second Starlink Satellite Has Inexplicably Exploded in Orbit — And Nobody Knows Why
stories.hook: SpaceX's 7,000-satellite constellation just had another mysterious self-detonation, with no collision and no clear explanation.
stories.body:
Starlink-34343 broke apart on March 29 at an altitude of 560 kilometers, less than a year after it launched. Ground-based tracking firm LeoLabs identified at least 17 debris fragments and attributed the event to an 'internal energetic source' — meaning something inside the satellite caused the explosion, not a strike from external debris. This is the second such unexplained Starlink breakup in recent months.

SpaceX issued a statement saying the event poses 'no risk' to the International Space Station or the Artemis II crew. But astrophysicist Jonathan McDowell publicly disputed that characterization, noting that newly created debris clouds at low orbital altitudes can pose collision risks for years as fragments gradually de-orbit.

With over 7,000 Starlink satellites currently in orbit — and thousands more planned — the mystery of why these spacecraft are spontaneously breaking apart carries real consequences for everyone who uses the sky. Each explosion adds to the debris environment that all other operators, including NASA, must navigate. The two unexplained incidents have reignited calls for greater transparency about satellite anomalies before the constellation grows further.

image.alt: A satellite against a dark background in orbit
image.credit: Photo by NASA Hubble Space Telescope on Unsplash

links.title: The Verge: Another Starlink satellite has inexplicably exploded
Scientific American: SpaceX Starlink satellite suffers mysterious anomaly

stories.headline: Hedgehogs Can Hear Ultrasound — And That Might Save Them From Cars
stories.hook: The spiky garden visitor shuffling through your hedges has been hiding a superpower the whole time.
stories.body:
A new study in the journal Biology Letters has confirmed for the first time that European hedgehogs can hear frequencies up to at least 85 kHz — well into the ultrasonic range. Humans max out at around 20 kHz. The hedgehogs' peak sensitivity sits near 40 kHz, a frequency range used by bats for echolocation and by certain pest-deterrent devices marketed to gardeners.

The anatomy is the key to the secret. Hedgehog middle-ear bones are unusually small and dense, with a fused joint that makes the entire auditory chain stiffer than in most mammals — precisely the configuration that enables transmission of very high-frequency vibrations. Scientists from Oxford, Copenhagen, and Aarhus collaborated on the finding after years of speculation about why hedgehogs seemed to react to sounds humans couldn't hear.

The practical payoff could be significant. European hedgehog populations have declined sharply in recent decades, and road deaths are a leading cause. Researchers say the discovery opens the door to ultrasonic roadside deterrents — devices inaudible to humans, dogs, and most other wildlife — that could warn hedgehogs away from road edges at night. A creature that seemed entirely ordinary turns out to have been perceiving a dimension of sound we've been missing entirely.

image.alt: A white hedgehog on green grass
image.credit: Photo by Artur Stanulevich on Unsplash

links.title: University of Oxford: Ultrasound could help save hedgehogs
Smithsonian Magazine: Hedgehog hearing discovery

stories.headline: Hummingbirds Drink the Equivalent of a Cocktail Every Day — And Never Get Drunk
stories.hook: Pollinators have been quietly microdosing alcohol for millions of years, and they evolved to handle it just fine.
stories.body:
UC Berkeley researchers tested nectar from 29 flowering plant species and found detectable alcohol in at least 26 of them. The culprit: yeasts fermenting the sugars in flower nectar, producing ethanol concentrations that range from traces up to about 2%. Because hummingbirds consume 50 to 150 percent of their body weight in nectar every single day, an Anna's hummingbird ingests roughly 0.2 grams of ethanol per kilogram of body weight daily — equivalent, proportionally, to one human drink.

Despite this, the birds show zero signs of intoxication. They happily drink nectar at up to around 1% alcohol concentration, but start avoiding it when concentrations climb above roughly 2%. The study, published in Royal Society Open Science, suggests hummingbirds have evolved a calibrated tolerance — and possibly a preference — for low-level alcohol in their diet.

The implications reach beyond hummingbirds. Bees were also found to consume alcoholic nectar regularly. Across the animal kingdom, the relationship between natural fermentation and pollinator behavior appears to be far more ancient and widespread than previously understood. The flowers may even have evolved yeast-friendly conditions that produce just enough alcohol to make their nectar more attractive without repelling their visitors — a co-evolutionary relationship that's been unfolding since long before fermentation was a human invention.

image.alt: A hummingbird hovering near a flower
image.credit: Photo by Richard Sagredo on Unsplash

links.title: Berkeley News: Birds and bees sip alcohol
The Register: Pollinators and natural alcohol

stories.headline: Scientists Cracked a Decades-Old Mystery: Malaria Parasites Have Tiny Rocket Engines Inside Them
stories.hook: A parasite that kills 600,000 people a year has been running microscopic rocket engines this whole time — and nobody knew.
stories.body:
Inside malaria-causing Plasmodium parasites are iron crystals called hemozoin that have spun and tumbled under microscopes for decades without anyone understanding why. Researchers at the University of Utah have finally solved it, and the answer is stranger than anyone guessed: the crystals are self-propelled, powered by the exact same chemistry used to propel spacecraft.

Hydrogen peroxide breaks down inside the parasite, releasing oxygen that drives the crystals in a manner directly analogous to how hydrogen peroxide rocket thrusters work. When researchers reduced available oxygen in the environment, the crystals slowed to roughly half their normal speed. It's the first confirmed case of a self-propelled metallic nanoparticle in any biological system.

The function appears to be housekeeping: Plasmodium parasites generate toxic amounts of hydrogen peroxide and free iron as byproducts of breaking down hemoglobin for food. The spinning hemozoin crystals help clear the peroxide and safely sequester the dangerous iron. Understanding this molecular engine in detail opens new avenues for drug development — interfering with how the parasite manages its own toxic waste could be a route to treatments that overcome existing antimalarial resistance.

image.alt: A microscope close-up of biological cells
image.credit: Photo by Alexander Zabrodskiy on Unsplash

links.title: SciTechDaily: The bizarre malaria mystery solved
University of Utah Health: Malaria parasite iron crystals

stories.headline: A Cow Named Veronika Just Became the First of Her Species Ever Documented Using Tools
stories.hook: She figured it out herself, she's 13 years old, and she was never trained — and she just rewrote what we thought we knew about animal intelligence.
stories.body:
Veronika is a 13-year-old Swiss Brown cow living on an organic farm in Austria. Researchers observing her behavior noticed something unprecedented: she was using a brush to scratch herself, choosing the bristle side for her back and switching to the smoother handle end for more sensitive areas. It's precisely this kind of flexible, multi-purpose tool use — adapting the same object for different functions in different contexts — that has long been considered a hallmark of advanced cognition.

Published in Current Biology, the study marks the first confirmed case of flexible tool use in cattle. Crucially, Veronika was never trained. She appears to have worked it out through independent problem-solving over years of living as a companion animal with access to farm equipment. The fact that she distinguishes between parts of the brush and adjusts her behavior accordingly rules out simple accident or conditioning.

Tool use has traditionally been associated with primates, corvids, dolphins, and octopuses — species with large brains and strong social learning. Cattle were simply assumed to lack the cognitive flexibility. Veronika's case suggests that livestock intelligence may be routinely underestimated, with consequences not just for animal welfare but for how we think about the evolution of cognition itself.

image.alt: A black cow grazing on a green grass field
image.credit: Photo by Ali Elliott on Unsplash

links.title: Scientific American: First flexible tool use in a cow
Smithsonian Magazine: Cow named Veronika uses a broom

stories.headline: Scientists Found a Completely New Branch of Life — In the Ocean Zone Being Targeted for Mining
stories.hook: Researchers just added a new superfamily to the tree of life at the bottom of the Pacific, in the exact region where industrial mining is scheduled to begin.
stories.body:
The Clarion-Clipperton Zone (CCZ) is a 6-million-square-kilometer swath of the deep Pacific Ocean floor between Hawaii and Mexico. It's also the world's richest known deposit of polymetallic nodules — golf-ball-sized rocks loaded with cobalt, nickel, and manganese that battery manufacturers covet for electric vehicle production. Scientists have been cataloging its inhabitants in parallel with the mining industry's planning, and the new species keep arriving faster than the debate does.

The latest tally includes 24 new amphipod species — tiny crustaceans that thrive in the crushing pressure and darkness of the deep sea. The headline find is not just a species but an entire new superfamily: Mirabestioidea, and its associated new family Mirabestiidae. Adding a superfamily to the animal tree of life is genuinely rare. The lead researcher described it as 'a discovery we will all remember.' The paper was published in ZooKeys, and researchers estimate that over 90% of CCZ species remain unnamed.

The tension is stark: the biological wealth of this region is only beginning to reveal itself at precisely the moment industrial machinery is poised to scrape the seafloor where these organisms live. Recovery timescales for disturbed deep-sea ecosystems are measured in centuries, not years.

image.alt: Underwater photograph of the deep blue ocean
image.credit: Photo by Jeremy Bishop on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: New deep-sea species in CCZ
Inside Climate News: Scientists discover new deep-sea creatures
ZME Science: New species in deep-sea mining zone

stories.headline: A Particle Accelerator Just Decoded a 2,200-Year-Old Star Map Hidden Under Six Layers of Medieval Ink
stories.hook: The oldest known star catalog in history was hidden inside a 10th-century prayer book, waiting for the right machine to find it.
stories.body:
Hipparchus of Nicaea — credited as one of the founders of trigonometry and the creator of the first known systematic star catalog — left almost no surviving original work. His measurements, made around 130 BC, were known only through references in the writings of others. For centuries, historians suspected some of his text might be buried inside later manuscripts that had been erased and written over. They were right.

Using a synchrotron particle accelerator at SLAC National Lab, researchers fired X-rays at pages of the Codex Climaci Rescriptus, a palimpsest from St. Catherine's Monastery in Sinai now held at the Museum of the Bible. The X-rays revealed that the calcium-rich ink of the original text — Hipparchus's stellar coordinates — had been scraped away in the 9th or 10th century by medieval scribes who then wrote over it with iron-based ink. The two ink chemistries respond differently to X-ray fluorescence, making the older layer legible again for the first time in a thousand years.

The recovered text includes stellar positions accurate enough to be identifiable and to settle a long-running debate about whether Ptolemy plagiarized Hipparchus. The answer: Ptolemy used Hipparchus as a reference but also incorporated other independent sources — a more nuanced picture than either the plagiarist or original-genius narrative.

image.alt: A circular astronomical diagram showing celestial bodies and zodiac signs
image.credit: Photo by The New York Public Library on Unsplash

links.title: Scientific American: Lost ancient Greek star catalog decoded
Science News: Hipparchus star catalog recovered

stories.headline: Neanderthals Were on the Brink of Extinction for 350,000 Years Before We Arrived
stories.hook: Our closest relatives spent most of their existence teetering on the edge of oblivion — long before Homo sapiens ever appeared in Europe.
stories.body:
A major new genetic study from FAU Erlangen-Nürnberg has confirmed that Neanderthals, a species once distributed across a range stretching from northern Germany to Siberia, collapsed to a tiny remnant population in southwestern France roughly 65,000 years ago. A sustained cold, dry climate period drove them out of most of their territory. Virtually all later Neanderthals — including those whose DNA survives in living humans today — descended from this single isolated population.

The bottleneck wasn't a brief crisis. For hundreds of thousands of years before this final collapse, Neanderthals had been living in scattered, isolated pockets with significant inbreeding, their genetic diversity steadily eroding. The researchers analyzed mitochondrial DNA from dozens of specimens and found the signatures of a species perpetually near the edge — not a thriving lineage suddenly cut short, but one that had been biologically fragile for geological ages.

Neanderthals didn't go fully extinct until roughly 42,000 years ago, about 3,000 years after modern humans arrived in Europe. The overlap was brief, and the interbreeding that occurred (most people of non-African ancestry carry about 2% Neanderthal DNA) happened in this final chapter. The picture that emerges is less of a clash of civilizations and more of a fragile remnant population finally absorbed and outcompeted by a more numerous and robust species.

image.alt: The interior of a dark rocky cave with dramatic light
image.credit: Photo by Vincent Foret on Unsplash

links.title: FAU Erlangen-Nürnberg: Neanderthals and the bottleneck theory
Earth.com: Neanderthals on the brink for 350,000 years
Science: Neanderthals survived on a knife's edge

stories.headline: Scientists Grew a Working Human Oesophagus in a Lab — Using the Patient's Own Cells
stories.hook: Children born without a food pipe may soon receive one grown from their own body, with no immune suppression drugs required.
stories.body:
A team at Great Ormond Street Hospital (GOSH) and University College London has engineered a functional oesophagus using an approach that sidesteps the biggest challenge in transplant medicine: rejection. They started with a donor pig scaffold — the structural collagen framework of a pig oesophagus, stripped of every pig cell through a process called decellularization — then repopulated it with smooth muscle cells grown from a small biopsy of the intended recipient.

In pig transplant models, all five animals that received the engineered organ survived the full six-month study period. They developed functioning muscle layers, nerve connections, and blood vessel networks — and they could swallow. None required immunosuppression drugs, because the organ contained only the recipient's own biology on a neutral scaffold.

Published in Nature Biotechnology, the research targets a specific patient population: children born with oesophageal atresia, a congenital condition where the oesophagus is incomplete or absent. Current treatments are complex, often requiring repeated surgeries throughout childhood. The GOSH team believes human clinical trials could begin within five years. It is one of the cleaner examples of tissue engineering fulfilling the promise it made several decades ago.

image.alt: Medical professionals working in a laboratory environment
image.credit: Photo by National Cancer Institute on Unsplash

links.title: GOSH: Team engineers first lab-grown oesophagus
Nature: Lab-grown oesophagus research
MedicalXpress: Tissue engineering for children

stories.headline: Google's TurboQuant Shrinks AI Memory by 6x — With Zero Accuracy Loss and No Retraining
stories.hook: The biggest practical barrier to deploying AI just got knocked down by an algorithm that requires no changes to existing models.
stories.body:
Google Research's TurboQuant algorithm, formally presented at ICLR 2026 this week, compresses the 'key-value caches' that large language models use to track context during a conversation — one of the primary memory bottlenecks in AI deployment. The technique squeezes those caches down to 3.5 bits per channel, reducing memory requirements by approximately six times. On Nvidia H100 GPUs, the team demonstrated inference speeds up to eight times faster than baseline.

The key headline is what isn't lost: accuracy. Across standard benchmarks, TurboQuant showed no measurable degradation in model quality. And crucially, no fine-tuning or retraining is required. The algorithm is applied after the fact, to any existing model, without touching the weights.

Within 24 hours of the paper's release, developers had already ported it to Apple Silicon (via the MLX framework) and llama.cpp, the popular CPU inference engine. For AI infrastructure operators, the implications are direct: the same hardware can now serve roughly six times as many simultaneous conversations for the same memory cost. For anyone running local models, the gains in speed and accessibility are equally real. Memory has been the quiet bottleneck behind much of the AI deployment conversation; TurboQuant just made it substantially smaller.

image.alt: An abstract visualization of neural network nodes and connections
image.credit: Photo by Google DeepMind on Unsplash

links.title: Google Research Blog: TurboQuant
Tom's Hardware: TurboQuant compresses LLM caches
VentureBeat: 8x AI memory speedup

stories.headline: Intel Got Caught Specifically Targeting and Rewriting a Benchmark to Inflate Its Scores
stories.hook: This isn't optimization — it's a CPU manufacturer that identified a benchmark by its checksum and transformed it before it could run.
stories.body:
Geekbench, one of the most widely used CPU benchmark suites, has published a detailed technical analysis showing that Intel's Binary Optimization Tool (BOT) — quietly included with Arrow Lake Plus CPUs — identifies the Geekbench 6 executable by its cryptographic checksum, then rewrites its machine code to use vectorized instructions before the benchmark runs. The result: score increases of up to 40% on individual workloads and around 8% on overall scores.

Intel had publicly disclosed that BOT performs some forms of code optimization, including instruction reordering. But the aggressive vectorization that Geekbench's reverse engineering uncovered goes well beyond what was described. The company's official framing characterized BOT as a way to help 'legacy applications' that weren't compiled with modern instruction sets — not a tool that hunts for benchmark executables by hash and surgically enhances them.

Geekbench 6.7, released this week, now detects when BOT has modified its executable and marks those results as invalid. The broader issue, as hardware review sites have noted, is that Intel is hardly alone in the history of hardware benchmark gaming — but this is one of the cleaner documented cases of a manufacturer's tool specifically fingerprinting a known benchmark and treating it differently from real-world software.

image.alt: A CPU chip on a computer motherboard
image.credit: Photo by A L on Unsplash

links.title: Geekbench Blog: Analyzing Geekbench 6 under Intel's BOT
How-To Geek: Intel is faking CPU benchmark tests
Tom's Hardware: Geekbench investigates score jump

stories.headline: Robot Laboratories Are Running Science on Autopilot — 6,000 Experiments in Five Months
stories.hook: The scientific method itself is being automated, and these machines explore hypotheses that no human researcher would have thought to test.
stories.body:
Argonne National Laboratory's 'self-driving lab' ran more than 6,000 battery chemistry experiments in five months — work that would have taken years with traditional human-directed research. The system operates in a closed loop: AI designs the experiment, robots execute it, sensors collect the data, machine learning analyzes the results, and the AI uses those results to design the next experiment. No human intervention required between cycles.

A Nature feature this week surveying the field highlighted Sandia National Laboratories' autonomous system, which independently discovered a novel strategy for controlling light emission and demonstrated a fourfold improvement over existing approaches. A review paper in Royal Society Open Science notes that these systems don't just work faster — they traverse hypothesis spaces that human researchers, constrained by intuition and working hours, would simply never explore.

The philosophical implications run deeper than efficiency. Science has always been a human cognitive activity — framing questions, recognizing anomalies, making intuitive leaps. Self-driving labs invert this: they systematically exhaust possibility spaces without needing to understand what they're doing in any meaningful sense. Whether that's a new kind of science or a very sophisticated literature search is genuinely contested among researchers in the field.

image.alt: A laboratory filled with automated machinery and equipment
image.credit: Photo by ZHENYU LUO on Unsplash

links.title: Nature: The rise of self-driving labs
Argonne National Laboratory: Self-driving lab transforms materials discovery
Royal Society: Autonomous self-driving laboratories review

stories.headline: DDR5 RAM Has Tripled in Price Because AI Data Centers Are Eating the World's Memory Supply
stories.hook: Building a gaming PC just got significantly more expensive, and the cause is something most builders never see coming: AI.
stories.body:
DDR5 RAM prices have multiplied three to four times over the past six months, and the cause has nothing to do with typical supply-demand cycles. Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron — the three companies that manufacture most of the world's memory — have steadily shifted production capacity away from standard consumer DRAM and toward HBM (High Bandwidth Memory), the specialized stacked memory chips used in AI accelerators like Nvidia's H100 and H200. Consumer and enterprise DDR5 supply has contracted as a direct result.

The shortage isn't expected to be temporary. Analysts don't forecast meaningful relief until late 2027, when new fabrication capacity comes online. The AI memory demand is structural, not cyclical: as data centers continue scaling their GPU clusters, demand for HBM grows with every new training run and every new inference deployment.

There was a brief respite: when Google released TurboQuant this week — the algorithm that compresses AI memory by 6x — memory futures briefly dropped around 30% on the speculation that less physical memory would be needed per GPU. That dip is already reversing. The two markets — consumer PC builders and hyperscale AI infrastructure — now directly compete for the same pool of silicon manufacturing capacity, and for the foreseeable future, the data centers are winning.

image.alt: Four DDR RAM sticks laid on a wooden surface
image.credit: Photo by Andrey Matveev on Unsplash

links.title: IDC: Global memory shortage market analysis
Tom's Hardware: RAM price index 2026
NotebookCheck: DDR5 price spike analysis
